current_fact.fact_text: On 5 April 2009, North Korea launched its Kwangmyŏngsŏng‑2 rocket, claiming it carried a communications satellite while many other nations viewed it as a ballistic missile test. The launch, which passed over Japan, sparked urgent diplomatic tensions and highlighted how space technology can blur the line between scientific ambition and geopolitical provocation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

current_fact.match_reason: A contested "communications satellite" and the politics of connectivity resonate with an episode about our complex relationship to technology, networks, and invisible signals linking us.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1958, engineers detonated one of the largest non‑nuclear explosions in history to obliterate Ripple Rock, a deadly seamount lurking just below the surface in Canada’s Seymour Narrows. The blast literally reshaped the seafloor to make one of the world’s most treacherous shipping passages safer.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: An underwater mountain blown apart to protect sailors pairs with an episode about immersion and the hidden world beneath the waves, turning a dangerous seamount into a story of safer seas.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1942, Japanese carrier aircraft launched a surprise attack on Colombo in Ceylon (now Sri Lanka) as part of the Indian Ocean raid, sinking two British cruisers and damaging port facilities. For a brief moment, the Indian Ocean became a stage for long‑range naval aviation, showing how far sea‑borne air power could reach.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A sudden carrier‑borne strike that jolted the Indian Ocean echoes the "electric shock" theme and high‑voltage guitars, translating the shock of surprise into sonic form.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1879, Bolivia declared war on Chile and Chile responded by declaring war on both Bolivia and Peru, igniting the War of the Pacific. The conflict reshaped South America’s map, deciding which country would control rich nitrate deposits and even which nation had a coastline.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A brutal resource‑driven war over borders and power fits with a fiercely political rock episode about resistance, exploitation, and the costs of state decisions.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 2007, the Greek cruise ship MS Sea Diamond struck a volcanic reef near the island of Nea Kameni in the Santorini caldera and sank the following day. The wreck, now resting on the seafloor of a partially submerged volcano, became an eerie reminder of how geology and tourism can collide in dramatic fashion.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A cruise ship sinking in a Greek volcanic bay pairs naturally with an episode that sails sonically around the Mediterranean coastline, from Greek harbors to neighboring shores.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1991, Atlantic Southeast Airlines Flight 2311, an Embraer EMB‑120 commuter plane, crashed near Brunswick, Georgia, killing all 23 people on board, including U.S. Senator John Tower and NASA astronaut Sonny Carter. Investigators later traced the disaster to a propeller control failure, underscoring how tiny mechanical flaws can have enormous human consequences.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A tragic crash involving a spacefarer invites an introspective episode about bodily vulnerability, recovery, and the fragile line between motion and forced stillness.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1971, the Janatha Vimukthi Peramuna, a Marxist–Leninist group in Sri Lanka, launched an armed insurrection against the United Front government. The youth‑led revolt, unexpectedly widespread and intense, shook the island and exposed deep social and generational fractures.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: A radical leftist youth uprising aligns with an episode centered on Residente’s art of resistance and political critique, both exploring rebellion against entrenched systems.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1965, the film adaptation of the musical "My Fair Lady" won the Academy Award for Best Picture at the 37th Oscars. The lavish production turned a stage story about language, class, and transformation into a cinematic spectacle that still shapes how many people imagine Edwardian London.

episode.title: Morning Drops #50 - Postcards from Earth-London
episode.description:
Postcards from Earth-London is a Morning Drops walk through the city Gaia has called home for the past ten years, a place made of sound, memory, and late-night streets. 

Four tracks guide the journey: from iconic landmarks to quieter corners, from romance to reality checks, and back to the reason the city still feels alive: music.

queue.match_reason: A quintessentially London film about accent and class connects directly to an audio walk through modern London, contrasting cinematic myths with lived city soundscapes.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1942, Adolf Hitler issued Führer Directive No. 41, laying out the plan for Case Blue, the 1942 German offensive toward the Caucasus oilfields and Stalingrad. The document turned resource hunger into a ruthless blueprint, setting the stage for one of history’s most devastating urban battles.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A cold, expansionist war plan is set against an episode that chooses a radically different kind of destination: a collective, imaginative voyage through sound rather than conquest.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1936, an F5 tornado tore through Tupelo, Mississippi as part of the Tupelo–Gainesville outbreak, killing at least 233 people in one of the deadliest tornadoes in U.S. history. The storm struck overnight and destroyed entire neighborhoods, long before modern radar and warning systems existed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A catastrophic storm and the vulnerability it exposed pair with an episode built around rain, resilience, and the reminder that even the most violent weather eventually passes.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1945, Yugoslav leader Josip Broz Tito signed an agreement allowing the "temporary entry" of Soviet troops into Yugoslav territory to help liberate it from Nazi occupation. The move was a pragmatic alliance that would later give way to Tito’s famously independent path between East and West.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: A mountain country negotiating between empires aligns with an episode about mountain landscapes and navigating between different cultures and voices, each choosing their own path.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1818, Chilean independence forces under Bernardo O'Higgins and José de San Martín won a decisive victory over Spanish royalists at the Battle of Maipú. The battle effectively secured Chile’s independence and showed how coordinated liberation movements could reshape an entire continent.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A pivotal independence victory in South America mirrors an episode about Afrobeat as a musical language of post‑colonial freedom and political awakening on another continent.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1614, Pocahontas, a Powhatan woman, married English colonist John Rolfe in Virginia. The marriage was used as a political symbol of peace between English settlers and Native peoples, yet it also encapsulates a complex story of power, adaptation, and identity at the edge of two worlds.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

queue.match_reason: A famous, politically charged marriage becomes a lens on vulnerability, love, and power, fitting an episode devoted to love’s complicated, fragile side.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1998, Japan opened the Akashi Kaikyō Bridge to traffic, unveiling the world’s longest central span suspension bridge at nearly two kilometers. Engineers even had to account for Earth’s curvature and the flexing of its massive cables under wind, tide, and temperature.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: Japan’s record‑breaking, ultra‑precise bridge contrasts beautifully with an episode about wabi‑sabi, reminding us that even in a country famed for engineering perfection, there’s a parallel philosophy that honors the imperfect.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1951, Ethel and Julius Rosenberg were sentenced to death in the United States for passing atomic secrets to the Soviet Union. Their trial became one of the most controversial episodes of the Cold War, blending espionage, fear, and questions about justice into a single case.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A case about hidden atomic secrets and state control mirrors an episode about a hidden studio tool that quietly reshaped sound, raising questions about authenticity, concealment, and manipulation.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1621, the Mayflower, having delivered its passengers to the New World months earlier, set sail from Plymouth, Massachusetts on its return voyage to England. The ship that had carried a small group of settlers into the unknown now sailed back across the Atlantic almost empty, its passengers’ experiment only just beginning.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: An iconic Atlantic crossing in a wooden ship fits seamlessly with an episode recorded aboard a modern sailboat, both telling stories of life at sea and shared journeys over long water.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 919, the Fatimid heir al‑Qa'im bi‑Amr Allah departed from Raqqada to begin the second Fatimid invasion of Egypt, a bold attempt to wrest the Nile valley from Abbasid control. The campaign showed how shifting religious and political currents in North Africa could spill across deserts and reshape the map of the Islamic world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #02 - Waves of the Maghreb
episode.description: Oriental vibes guided by Yasmin. Discovering curious facts about the Maghreb, together with its fascinating music! A wonderful trip with a selection of songs from North Africa.
songs: Nass El Ghiwane - El Madi Fate

queue.match_reason: A medieval North African campaign aimed at Egypt and beyond pairs with an episode dedicated to Maghreb culture and music, turning historical conquest routes into sonic journeys.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 2018, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents raided a slaughterhouse in Grainger County, Tennessee, arresting nearly 100 undocumented Hispanic workers in one of the largest workplace raids in modern U.S. history. The operation fractured families overnight and spotlighted how immigration policy plays out on factory floors far from national capitals.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

queue.match_reason: A raid targeting Hispanic workers resonates with an episode exploring reggaeton’s Latin roots, giving cultural presence and voice to communities often reduced to statistics in immigration debates.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 2001 (commemorated later at the bridge’s opening in 1998), the Akashi Kaikyō Bridge’s design famously accounted for the 1995 Kobe earthquake, which shifted the location of its towers by nearly a meter during construction. Rather than scrapping the project, engineers adjusted the plans, turning seismic chaos into a design constraint.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Engineers learning from an earthquake and iterating a design to build a record‑breaking bridge mirrors an episode about learning through difficulty, adjustment, and persistence over time.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 1942’s broader context, the technology of radar—still relatively new—was being rapidly refined by all sides in World War II, turning invisible radio waves into early‑warning eyes. This quiet arms race in physics would soon accelerate into peacetime uses like air traffic control and weather forecasting.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: The birth of radar as an invisible, guiding technology parallels Sedna FM’s own first broadcast—an unseen signal carrying voices and rhythms out into space from a new station.

queue.fact_text: On 5 April 2009’s regional context, Japan’s sophisticated missile‑tracking network followed the North Korean launch from liftoff to splashdown, relying on a web of sensors originally nurtured by the same precision that built structures like the Akashi Kaikyō Bridge. It’s a reminder that engineering talent can illuminate or escalate regional tensions, depending on how it’s used.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: East Asian tensions and high‑tech tracking sit alongside an episode about Southeast Asian practice, movement, and cross‑cultural flows, both anchored in the Pacific Rim but carrying very different energies.

queue.fact_text: Across many of the 5 April events, from war directives to bridge openings and spaceflights, one thread is the quiet labor of unnamed scientists, builders, and artists whose work makes the headlines possible. Their stories rarely fit into a single date, yet they shape the infrastructures, instruments, and songs that define an era.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

queue.match_reason: An overview fact honoring the unseen, continuous work behind single‑day events pairs with a mellow, ongoing jazz groove—less about peaks and more about the long, sustaining flow over time.

published.fact_text: On 5 April 2010, Space Shuttle Discovery lifted off on mission STS-131, carrying a multi-purpose logistics module packed with science experiments, spare parts, and supplies for the International Space Station. The flight highlighted how orbiting laboratories rely on meticulously choreographed resupply runs to stay habitable and productive for months at a time.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal trip into space pairs with an episode about "Space Shift" and using empty space as creative potential, echoing the ISS as a blank lab orbiting Earth.

published.fact_text: On 5 April 1991, Space Shuttle Atlantis launched on STS-37 to deploy the Compton Gamma Ray Observatory, one of NASA’s Great Observatories. For nearly a decade, it mapped the sky in high‑energy gamma rays, revealing violent events like supernovae, black holes, and mysterious gamma‑ray bursts invisible to human eyes.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A telescope studying cosmic gamma-ray "crescendos" fits an evening mix that starts atmospheric and builds into energetic waves, mirroring how Compton slowly uncovered the high‑energy universe.

published.fact_text: On 5 April 2009, North Korea launched its Kwangmyŏngsŏng‑2 rocket, claiming it carried a communications satellite while many other nations viewed it as a ballistic missile test. The launch, which passed over Japan, sparked urgent diplomatic tensions and highlighted how space technology can blur the line between scientific ambition and geopolitical provocation.

episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.
